subject: EU Law
lesson_title: The Court of Justice of the EU and Other Institutions

questions.question: The Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU) as established by the Lisbon Treaty comprises:

options.A: One court: the Court of Justice
options.B: Two courts: the Court of Justice and the General Court
options.C: Three courts: the Court of Justice, the General Court, and the Civil Service Tribunal
options.D: One court: the European Court of Justice

questions.explanation: Following the Lisbon Treaty and the dissolution of the Civil Service Tribunal in 2016, the CJEU now comprises two courts: the Court of Justice (formerly 'European Court of Justice' or 'ECJ') and the General Court (formerly the Court of First Instance). Cases from the General Court may be appealed to the Court of Justice on points of law.

questions.question: The Court of Justice of the EU has jurisdiction in which of the following cases?

options.A: Preliminary references from national courts, infringement proceedings, and appeals from the General Court
options.B: Criminal trials of EU officials, commercial disputes between private parties, and treaty revision
options.C: Direct actions by EU citizens against member state governments in national law matters
options.D: Appeals against decisions of national supreme courts on EU law matters

questions.explanation: The Court of Justice's main jurisdictions are: preliminary references (Article 267 TFEU) from national courts; infringement proceedings (Articles 258-260 TFEU) against member states; actions for annulment against EU acts (Article 263 TFEU); actions for failure to act (Article 265 TFEU); and appeals from the General Court on points of law.

questions.question: The General Court (formerly the Court of First Instance) has jurisdiction primarily in:

options.B: Direct actions by individuals and companies against EU institutions (e.g., competition law appeals, staff cases, state aid decisions)
options.D: Interpretation of EU primary law (Treaties and Charter)

questions.explanation: The General Court handles direct actions by natural and legal persons against EU institutions — particularly competition law (appeals against Commission decisions fining companies), state aid cases, access to documents, and EU trademark/design cases. Its decisions can be appealed to the Court of Justice on points of law.

questions.question: The Court of Justice sits in various formations. Grand Chamber formation (15 judges) is used for:

options.A: All preliminary reference cases regardless of importance
options.B: Cases of particular legal importance or complexity, where a member state or institution is a party and requests Grand Chamber
options.C: All infringement proceedings against member states
options.D: All cases involving a challenge to EU institutional acts

questions.explanation: The Court of Justice can sit as a full Court (rare, for exceptional cases), Grand Chamber (15 judges — cases of particular importance), chambers of 5 or 3 judges (routine cases). A member state or institution that is a party may request Grand Chamber formation.

questions.question: The Advocates General of the Court of Justice fulfil which function?

options.A: They sit as additional judges and vote on the outcome of each case
options.B: They deliver a reasoned Opinion in each case, acting with complete impartiality and independence, to assist the Court — though the Opinion is not binding
options.C: They represent the EU institutions in cases brought before the Court
options.D: They pre-screen cases to determine admissibility before they reach the full Court

questions.explanation: Article 252 TFEU: the Advocates General (currently 11) assist the Court by delivering reasoned, impartial Opinions in open court in all cases or in those assigned to them. The Opinion is not binding on the Court, but is highly influential and often cited. The AG Opinion typically precedes the Court's judgment.

questions.question: The European Central Bank (ECB), established by the Maastricht Treaty, is responsible for:

options.A: Fiscal policy across the eurozone, including setting tax rates
options.B: Monetary policy for the eurozone — primarily maintaining price stability
options.C: Financial regulation and supervision of all EU banks
options.D: Managing the EU's annual budget and external reserves

questions.explanation: The ECB (Article 282 TFEU) is responsible for monetary policy in the eurozone (the member states that have adopted the euro). Its primary objective is price stability (maintaining inflation below but close to 2%). Fiscal policy remains with member state governments (subject to EU fiscal coordination rules).

questions.question: The Court of Auditors is responsible for:

options.A: Adjudicating cases of fraud against the EU's financial interests
options.B: Examining the legality and regularity of EU revenue and expenditure, and auditing sound financial management
options.C: Setting the EU's annual budget in consultation with the Commission
options.D: Supervising the activities of the European Anti-Fraud Office (OLAF)

questions.explanation: The Court of Auditors (Article 285 TFEU) audits EU accounts and provides the Parliament and Council with a statement of assurance as to the reliability of the accounts and the legality and regularity of underlying transactions. It also issues annual reports and special reports but has no judicial powers — its name is misleading.

questions.question: The European Economic and Social Committee (EESC) and the Committee of the Regions (CoR) fulfil which institutional role?

options.A: Co-legislative bodies with equal power to the Parliament and Council in their respective areas
options.B: Advisory bodies that must be consulted in specified areas but whose opinions are not binding
options.C: Executive agencies responsible for implementing EU programmes
options.D: Supervisory bodies overseeing the Commission's exercise of delegated powers

questions.explanation: The EESC (representing employers, trade unions, and civil society) and CoR (representing regional and local authorities) are consultative bodies. The Treaties require consultation in specified policy areas. Their opinions are not binding — the Council and Parliament may act contrary to their advice — but provide institutional representation for non-governmental stakeholders.

questions.question: The principle of institutional balance in EU law means:

options.A: All EU institutions must have equal numbers of members from each member state
options.B: Each institution must act within the powers conferred on it by the Treaties, and may not encroach on the powers attributed to other institutions
options.C: EU budget resources must be distributed equally among all institutions
options.D: Large and small member states must have equal representation in all EU institutions

questions.explanation: The principle of institutional balance (confirmed by the Court in cases such as Roquette Frères v Council [1980]) requires that each institution act within its Treaty powers and respect the prerogatives of others. The Court has used it to annul acts that wrongly bypassed consultation obligations or used incorrect legal bases.

questions.question: The European Ombudsman's role is to:

options.A: Adjudicate disputes between EU citizens and member state governments
options.B: Investigate complaints of maladministration by EU institutions, bodies, offices, and agencies
options.C: Monitor compliance with the Charter of Fundamental Rights in member states
options.D: Oversee the conduct of Advocates General in Court of Justice proceedings

questions.explanation: The European Ombudsman (Article 228 TFEU) is elected by the European Parliament and investigates cases of maladministration in the activities of EU institutions, bodies, offices, or agencies (not including the Court of Justice in its judicial role). Any EU citizen or resident may lodge a complaint.

questions.question: The European Investment Bank (EIB) contributes to EU objectives by:

options.A: Setting interest rates for the eurozone
options.B: Providing long-term finance for investment projects in member states and third countries that serve EU policy objectives
options.C: Administering EU structural and cohesion funds
options.D: Regulating cross-border banking and financial services

questions.explanation: The EIB (Article 308 TFEU) is the EU's development bank, owned by the member states. It provides long-term loans and guarantees for capital investment projects that contribute to EU objectives — including infrastructure, SME support, climate action, and innovation. It is not an EU supervisory body.

questions.question: The principle of conferral, under Article 5 TEU, means:

options.A: The EU has general legislative competence across all policy areas
options.B: The Union acts only within the limits of the competences conferred upon it by the member states in the Treaties
options.C: Member states must confer additional powers on the EU when integration requires it
options.D: EU institutions have implied powers to act wherever necessary to achieve Treaty objectives

questions.explanation: Article 5(2) TEU: the principle of conferral limits the EU to acting 'only within the limits of the competences conferred upon it by the Member States in the Treaties to attain the objectives set out therein. Competences not conferred upon the Union in the Treaties remain with the Member States.'

questions.question: The General Court's jurisdiction was expanded by the 2015-2016 reform that abolished the Civil Service Tribunal. What additional cases did the General Court assume?

options.B: Staff cases — disputes between EU institutions and their employees
options.D: Appeals from national supreme courts in EU law matters

questions.explanation: The Civil Service Tribunal, which handled disputes between EU institutions and their employees ('staff cases'), was abolished and its work transferred to the General Court from 2016. This was part of a broader reform to increase the General Court's capacity and double the number of its judges.
